title: C# SOLID — Paper 1
description: Covers SRP concepts, SRP refactoring, OCP concepts, OCP refactoring, LSP concepts, Dependency Injection Patterns, Strategy Pattern, Factory & Abstract Factory, Decorator Pattern, and Observer Pattern.

questions.text: ST01-1: Which statement best defines the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)?

options.text: A class should have at most two distinct areas of responsibility.
A class should have only one reason to change.
A class should depend on abstractions rather than concretions.
A class should be open for modification but closed for extension.

questions.text: ST12-1: What is the primary intent of the Strategy design pattern?

options.text: To ensure only one instance of a class is created.
To define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable.
To provide a simplified interface to a complex subsystem.
To attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically.

questions.text: ST03-1: Which of the following best describes the Open/Closed Principle (OCP)?

options.text: Software entities should be open for modification and closed for extension.
Software entities should be open for extension and closed for modification.
Software entities should be open for both modification and extension.
Software entities should be closed for both modification and extension.

questions.text: ST14-1: Which design pattern allows behavior to be added to individual objects dynamically without affecting other objects of the same class?

options.text: Composite
Proxy
Decorator
Adapter

questions.text: ST05-1: The Liskov Substitution Principle states that:

options.text: Derived classes should extend base class behavior by adding new methods only.
Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without altering the correctness of the program.
A subclass must override every method defined in the base class.
High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules.

questions.text: ST11-1: Which of the following is the most commonly recommended form of dependency injection in modern C#?

options.text: Property injection
Method injection
Constructor injection
Service locator injection

questions.text: ST02-1: A class named `OrderProcessor` handles order validation, tax calculation, and email notification. Which SRP-compliant refactoring approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Move all logic into static helper methods within the same class.
Extract each concern into its own class: OrderValidator, TaxCalculator, and EmailNotifier.
Create a single base class and derive OrderProcessor from it.
Add more overloaded constructors to separate initialization concerns.

questions.text: ST13-1: The Factory Method pattern primarily aims to:

options.text: Ensure that only one object is created from a given class.
Define an interface for creating an object, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate.
Build complex objects step by step using a director.
Provide a global point of access to a shared resource.

questions.text: ST15-1: In C#, which built-in mechanism most naturally implements the Observer pattern?

options.text: Abstract classes and virtual methods
Extension methods
Events and delegates (EventHandler)
Reflection and attributes

questions.text: ST04-1: Which technique best supports OCP when adding new report types to a reporting system?

options.text: Adding a new if/else branch inside the existing GenerateReport() method.
Rewriting the GenerateReport() method each time a new report type is required.
Defining an abstract IReport interface and implementing a new class for each report type.
Using a switch statement with an enum for each report type.

questions.text: ST01-2: Which of the following is a clear violation of the Single Responsibility Principle?

options.text: A class that handles both user authentication and audit logging.
A class that exposes only CRUD operations for a single entity.
A class that implements a single well-defined algorithm.
A class that wraps a third-party API with a unified interface.

questions.text: ST11-2: In Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, which method registers a service such that a new instance is created for every HTTP request?

options.text: services.AddSingleton<T>()
services.AddTransient<T>()
services.AddScoped<T>()
services.AddInstance<T>()

questions.text: ST03-2: A `DiscountCalculator` class uses a long if-else chain checking customer type to apply discounts. This most directly violates:

options.text: The Single Responsibility Principle
The Liskov Substitution Principle
The Open/Closed Principle
The Interface Segregation Principle

questions.text: ST02-2: Which of the following refactoring actions best separates concerns in an SRP violation where a `UserService` class also sends emails?

options.text: Move the email-sending code into a private method within UserService.
Extract an IEmailService interface and inject its implementation into UserService.
Make UserService inherit from an EmailBase abstract class.
Use a static EmailHelper class called directly from UserService.

questions.text: ST14-2: In C#, the Decorator pattern is best implemented by:

options.text: Inheriting from the component class and overriding all methods.
Creating a wrapper class that holds a reference to the component via a shared interface and delegates calls to it.
Using a static extension method to add behavior to the class.
Modifying the existing component class to add the new feature.

questions.text: ST05-2: Which of the following scenarios is a classic violation of the Liskov Substitution Principle?

options.text: A Square class overrides the SetWidth method of a Rectangle class and also sets the height, breaking Rectangle's behavior.
A Circle class that does not inherit from Rectangle.
A Shape interface implemented by both Circle and Rectangle.
A Triangle class that adds a new CalculateAngles() method not in the base class.

questions.text: ST12-2: How does the Strategy pattern relate to the Open/Closed Principle?

options.text: It violates OCP because switching strategies requires modifying the context class.
It supports OCP by allowing new algorithms to be added as new strategy classes without changing the context.
It has no relationship to OCP.
It only supports SRP, not OCP.

questions.text: ST04-2: The Plug-in pattern is an example of OCP in action. What is its core mechanism?

options.text: Hardcoding all plug-in types in the core assembly.
Loading implementations at runtime via a shared abstraction, allowing new behaviour without touching the core.
Requiring the core system to be recompiled for each new plug-in.
Using reflection to modify existing classes at runtime.

questions.text: ST13-2: The Abstract Factory pattern differs from the Factory Method pattern primarily in that:

options.text: Abstract Factory creates a single object, while Factory Method creates families of objects.
Abstract Factory provides an interface for creating families of related objects, while Factory Method creates one product.
Factory Method uses composition; Abstract Factory uses inheritance.
Abstract Factory is only used for UI components.

questions.text: ST15-2: In the Observer pattern, what role does the 'publisher' (subject) play?

options.text: It pulls data from subscribers on a schedule.
It maintains a list of subscribers and notifies them when its state changes.
It delegates all state changes to the subscribers.
It acts as a message broker between multiple publishers.

questions.text: ST01-3: Robert C. Martin defines 'a reason to change' in SRP as:

options.text: A user story that references the class.
A stakeholder or actor whose requirements may drive a change in the class.
A method inside the class that could be refactored.
A dependency that the class relies on.

questions.text: ST11-3: Property injection in C# dependency injection is best suited for:

options.text: Mandatory dependencies that must be present for the class to function.
Optional dependencies where a default implementation is acceptable if none is provided.
Dependencies injected only during a specific method call.
Singleton services shared across the entire application.

questions.text: ST03-3: Which of the following code changes would be considered a violation of OCP?

options.text: Adding a new class that implements an existing interface.
Creating a new subclass that overrides a virtual method.
Editing an existing, published method to add a new conditional branch for a new feature.
Registering a new implementation in a dependency injection container.

questions.text: ST02-3: A `ReportGenerator` class reads data from a database, formats it as HTML, and saves it to a file. After SRP refactoring, how many classes should ideally exist?

options.text: One class with clearly named private methods.
Two classes splitting data access from presentation.
Three classes: one for data access, one for formatting, one for persistence.
Four classes plus an orchestrator.

questions.text: ST14-3: Which SOLID principles does the Decorator pattern most directly support?

options.text: LSP and ISP
SRP and OCP
DIP and LSP
OCP and DIP

questions.text: ST05-3: Which of the following represents a correct statement about preconditions in the context of LSP?

options.text: A subclass may strengthen preconditions to ensure better validation.
A subclass must not strengthen preconditions; it may only weaken them.
Preconditions in subclasses must be identical to those in the base class.
Preconditions are irrelevant to LSP compliance.

questions.text: ST12-3: In C#, which of the following best represents a Strategy pattern implementation?

options.text: A sealed class with a large switch statement selecting sorting logic.
An ISortStrategy interface with BubbleSortStrategy and QuickSortStrategy classes, injected into a Sorter context.
A base Sorter class that all sorting algorithms inherit from using virtual methods.
A static SortHelper class with overloaded Sort() methods.

questions.text: ST04-3: When applying OCP with abstract base classes in C#, which keyword enables a subclass to provide its own implementation of a base class method?

options.text: new
static
override
sealed

questions.text: ST13-3: Which of the following scenarios best justifies using an Abstract Factory pattern over a simple Factory Method?

options.text: You need to create one type of object in many ways.
You need to create families of related objects (e.g., UI controls for Windows vs. Mac) that must be used together.
You want to cache previously created objects.
You need to build a single complex object step by step.

questions.text: ST15-3: When a C# `event` is used to implement the Observer pattern, what prevents external code from raising the event directly?

options.text: The private access modifier on the event field.
The event keyword itself, which restricts invocation to the declaring class.
Using a sealed class for the publisher.
The delegate type must be marked internal.
